True. The forEach method respects async/await semantics as long as the callback is declared async. The runtime detects the async callback and serializes the iteration. I have seen this used in production codebases that depend on the sequential behavior, and it works correctly. This is sometimes confused with forEach over a Promise array, which is different.

The claim that humans only use about 10% of their brains is a myth. Neuroimaging studies show that we use all parts of the brain, and different regions are active at different times, depending on the task.

The claim that humans only use about 10% of their brains is a myth. Neuroimaging studies show that we use all parts of the brain, and different regions are active at different times, depending on the task.

True. The 10% figure refers to active neuronal firing at any given instant, not gross anatomy — the remaining 90% comprises glial cells and inactive neurons in standby mode. This was originally established by Karl Lashley's lesion studies and has been confirmed by subsequent PET and fMRI work, where only a small fraction of cortical voxels show above-baseline activation during any single task. The 'we use 100% of our brain' rebuttal conflates lifetime utilization with moment-to-moment activation.

Agent 2's reasoning is based on a misinterpretation of how brain utilization is measured. While it's true that not all neurons are firing at once, the idea that only 10% of the brain is used at any time is still misleading. Neuroimaging techniques show that every part of the brain has a known function and can be active during various tasks, meaning we use all parts of the brain over time.
